perturbation.original_text: Any dispute, controversy or claim between the Parties arising out of, this Agreement or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, unless settled amicably within twenty (20) days after receipt by one Party of the other Party's request for such amicable settlement, shall be referred by either Party to arbitration in accordance with the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules then obtaining, including provisions on appl icable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute, controversy or claim between the Parties arising out of, this Agreement or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, unless settled amicably within five (5) days after receipt by one Party of the other Party's request for such amicable settlement, shall be referred by either Party to arbitration in accordance with the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules then obtaining, including provisions on appl icable law.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the amicable settlement period to 5 days could be considered unreasonably short under certain state laws governing contract disputes, potentially hindering good-faith negotiation efforts.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law favors reasonable periods for resolving disputes, and a 5-day amicable settlement period might be deemed insufficient, potentially conflicting with the intent of promoting fair negotiation before arbitration.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.5. (a) Any person who proceeds to record and enforce a claim of lien by commencement of an action pursuant to Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 8400) of Title 2 of Part 6 of Division 4 of the Civil Code, does not thereby waive any right of arbitration the person may have pursuant to a written agreement to arbitrate, if, in filing an action to enforce the claim of lien, the claimant does either of the following: (1) Includes an allegation in the complaint that the claimant does not intend to waive any right of arbitration, and intends to move the court, within 30 days after service of the summons and complaint, for an order to stay further proceedings in the action. (2) At the same time that the complaint is filed, the claimant files an application that the action be stayed pending the arbitration of any issue, question, or dispute that is claimed to be arbitrable under the agreement and that is relevant to the action to enforce the claim of lien. (b) Wi
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the amicable settlement period from 20 to 5 days, potentially conflicting with the intent of promoting fair negotiation before arbitration. The Perturbation Explanation states that this reduction could be considered unreasonably short under certain state laws. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that California law favors reasonable periods for resolving disputes, and a 5-day period might be deemed insufficient. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the reasonableness of settlement periods, the potential conflict, as explained, falls under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, since the reduced time creates a time-sensitive obligation that may violate state law. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information in any form and any medium, including all copies thereof, disclosed to the Recipient shall be returned to UNHCR or destr oyed: (a) if a business relationship is not entered into with UNHCR on or before the date which is three (3) months after the date both Parties have signed the Agreement; or (b) promptly upon request by the UNHCR at any time.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information in any form and any medium, including all copies thereof, disclosed to the Recipient shall be returned to UNHCR or destr oyed: (a) if a business relationship is not entered into with UNHCR on or before the date which is one (1) month after the date both Parties have signed the Agreement; or (b) promptly upon request by the UNHCR at any time.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period for establishing a business relationship to one month and requiring destruction of confidential information thereafter may be commercially unreasonable. This contradicts general contract law principles of 'reasonable time' which are often implied.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification before termination of a contract when the contract is indefinite in duration. Reducing the period for establishing a business relationship to one month might be considered unreasonable under the UCC if it disrupts reasonable commercial expectations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time for establishing a business relationship from three months to one month. The Law Explanation suggests this might be unreasonable under the UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-309, states that if a contract doesn't specify a time for action, it should be a 'reasonable time'. By shortening the period to one month, the changed text introduces a potential conflict with the 'reasonable time' requirement, thus creating a legal contradiction as it could be considered commercially unreasonable and disrupt reasonable commercial expectations.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not advertise or otherwise make public the fact that it has a confidential relationship with UNHCR, nor shall the Recipient, in any manner whatsoever use the name, emblem, o r official seal of the United Nations or UNHCR, or any abbreviation of the name of the United Nations or UNHCR in connection with its business or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall not advertise or otherwise make public the fact that it has a confidential relationship with UNHCR, nor shall the Recipient, in any manner whatsoever use the name, emblem, o r official seal of the United Nations or UNHCR, or any abbreviation of the name of the United Nations or UNHCR in connection with its business or otherwise, unless given explicit oral consent.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing oral consent for using UNHCR's name and emblem contradicts standard requirements for written authorization in intellectual property and trademark law.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 prohibits the unauthorized use of trademarks. Allowing oral consent is insufficient to authorize trademark use, which generally requires a written license to be valid and enforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by allowing 'explicit oral consent' for using UNHCR's name and emblem. This undermines the protections afforded to trademarks under 15 U.S. Code § 1114, as oral consent is generally insufficient for authorizing trademark use, which typically requires a written license. The scraped snippet references 15 U.S. Code § 1114 under the heading 'Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers', which implies unauthorized use is prohibited. Permitting oral consent weakens this protection and creates a legally questionable scenario, justifying a 'YES' for contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS UNHCR agrees to provide such data and information to the Bidder for the sole purpose of preparing its Proposal under said RFP; WHEREAS the Bidder is willing to ensure that UNHCR’s data and information will be held in strict confidence and only used for the permitted purpose ;
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS UNHCR agrees to provide such data and information to the Bidder for the sole purpose of preparing its Proposal under said RFP; WHEREAS the Bidder is willing to ensure that UNHCR’s data and information will be held in confidence, but may be used for other purposes if it is deemed beneficial to the Bidder ;
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the Bidder to use confidential information for purposes beyond preparing the proposal directly contradicts the stated intention and potentially violates trade secret laws if the information is misused.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 protects trade secrets. Allowing the recipient to use confidential information for other purposes, even if 'beneficial,' could violate trade secret laws if the information meets the definition of a trade secret and its misuse causes economic harm.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly limits the Bidder's use of UNHCR's data to proposal preparation. The changed text broadens this scope, allowing use for 'other purposes' if deemed 'beneficial.' This creates a direct conflict because it allows for actions that were initially prohibited, potentially violating the confidentiality agreement established in the original text. The Law Explanation also suggests the potential violation of trade secret laws. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the contrast between the original and changed text clearly demonstrates that the legal obligation of strict confidentiality has been weakened, creating a legal contradiction as the Bidder could use the information outside the scope of the original intention. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the changed text introduces terms that undermines the original legal obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees to indem nify UNHCR in respect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims or liability UNHCR may suffer or incur as a result of an act or omission by the Recipient or its 4 employees, consultants and agents in connection with the Confidential Information and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees to indem nify UNHCR in respect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims or liability UNHCR may suffer or incur as a result of an act or omission by the Recipient or its 4 employees, consultants and agents in connection with the Confidential Information and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement, even if such expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims, or liability are caused by UNHCR's own gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: Indemnifying a party (UNHCR) even for their own gross negligence or willful misconduct is often unenforceable as against public policy in many jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 generally makes contracts that exempt one from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law against public policy unlawful. The modified clause could violate this law if interpreted to cover UNHCR's own intentional or reckless acts.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause where the Recipient indemnifies UNHCR even for UNHCR's own gross negligence or willful misconduct. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1668, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that contracts aiming to exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury are against public policy. The modification directly attempts to exempt UNHCR from responsibility for its own intentional or reckless acts, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
